title: To Cur With Love

jokes.text: Local inventors are vying for a million dollar stipend donated by T. Montgomery Burns, as part of a court settlement for causing one billion dollars of environmental damage.
jokes.setup: Burns is supposedly rewarding excellence in alternative energy
jokes.punchline: It's actually court-ordered restitution for massive environmental damage
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns character moment - turning court-ordered damages into self-aggrandizing publicity. Well-structured irony.

jokes.text: Shoo. Go have one of your enormous litters.
jokes.setup: Burns dismissing what appears to be a stray dog
jokes.punchline: Casual cruelty delivered with aristocratic disdain
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns characterization - casual cruelty delivered matter-of-factly. Economy of language is excellent.

jokes.text: Well, I've harnessed the ancient power of slavery to run the school bus of tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Presenting an alternative energy invention
jokes.punchline: Casually suggests using slavery as power source
jokes.explanation: Darkly subversive humor with perfect deadpan delivery. The juxtaposition of 'ancient power' and 'bus of tomorrow' adds layers.

jokes.text: Whose boy is this? I'll take him home. You'll take his place.
jokes.setup: Child appears to be lost or abandoned
jokes.punchline: Burns suggests literal human trafficking/slavery exchange
jokes.explanation: Escalates the slavery joke with Burns' characteristic evil. The casual delivery makes it funnier and more disturbing.

jokes.text: Some say they're powered by decaying nitrates, others by tiny insects beneath the hot dog skins.
jokes.setup: Apu explaining his perpetual motion hot dog machine
jokes.punchline: Reveals disgusting truth about gas station hot dogs
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about gas station food quality. Character-appropriate for Apu's scientific curiosity.

jokes.text: Hard to believe I once turned down a job in Tampa, Florida, because I believed in this city. That was 15 years ago. They wouldn't take you now.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy lamenting a missed opportunity
jokes.punchline: Brutal reality check that he's now unemployable
jokes.explanation: Well-structured setup and punchline with good character moment for Comic Book Guy's delusions of grandeur.

jokes.text: Frink Labs, a division of Allied Frink, the Good-Glavin people
jokes.setup: Professor Frink introducing his company
jokes.punchline: Corporate parody using his signature nonsense word 'glavin'
jokes.explanation: Perfect character integration using Frink's established verbal tics in a corporate parody context.

jokes.text: Oh, yes, it's the Moyvin-Hoyvin Vehicle.
jokes.setup: Asked what MHV stands for
jokes.punchline: Frink's nonsense-word explanation that reveals nothing
jokes.explanation: Classic Frink moment - using pseudo-scientific nonsense words as if they're legitimate explanations.

jokes.text: The brakes are powered by silence.
jokes.setup: Someone yells to step on the brakes
jokes.punchline: Reveals the fatal flaw in his sound-powered vehicle
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - the one thing needed to stop the vehicle is impossible to achieve. Perfect economy and structure.

jokes.text: Look out because of the zooming.
jokes.setup: Frink's vehicle is careening out of control
jokes.punchline: His characteristically awkward way of describing motion
jokes.explanation: Minor character moment showing Frink's odd way of expressing urgency.

jokes.text: I'm okay. I'll just go live with my son. No! No!
jokes.setup: Frink claims to be okay after the crash
jokes.punchline: His own declaration of moving in with his son horrifies him more than the crash
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - the prospect of living with family is worse than physical injury. Relatable family dynamics.

jokes.text: You know you can't lift your lifting belt without wearing your belt-lifting-belt.
jokes.setup: Homer can't lift something without his lifting belt
jokes.punchline: Reveals an absurd infinite regress of specialized belts
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist escalation showing Homer's over-reliance on safety equipment. The infinite regress is brilliantly silly.

jokes.text: Hold down the fort? With my bad back?
jokes.setup: Marge asks Homer to hold down the fort
jokes.punchline: Homer takes the idiom literally and complains about the physical demands
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer literal interpretation of idioms. Character-appropriate but not particularly original.

jokes.text: Just call me DeForest Kelley. I gotta write that down and send it to Conan.
jokes.setup: Homer deforesting his virtual village
jokes.punchline: Pun on Star Trek actor's name plus meta-reference to Conan O'Brien
jokes.explanation: Clever pun but relies on pop culture reference. Meta-element about writing for Conan adds a layer but feels slightly forced.

jokes.text: Tavern, tavern, tavern, tavern, tavern, tavern... Brothel. Parsonage.
jokes.setup: Homer building his medieval village
jokes.punchline: Excessive taverns followed by morally contradictory buildings
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - excessive drinking establishments plus the moral contradiction of brothel next to parsonage.

jokes.text: Yeah, that's right, your Lord sticks his hand in his pants. And yes, it is the same hand I tapped you with.
jokes.setup: Homer apparently talking to his virtual villagers
jokes.punchline: Reveals disturbing personal habits while playing
jokes.explanation: Characteristic Homer grossness but borders on too crude. The escalation works for character but lacks wit.

jokes.text: Did you spend this whole time doing fake chores in a fake village? It's real to them.
jokes.setup: Marge confronts Homer about wasting time gaming
jokes.punchline: Homer's sincere defense of his virtual villagers' reality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - prioritizing digital beings over real responsibilities. Great economy in the delivery.

jokes.text: But what if he got out through your half-completed tunnel to Flanders's fridge?
jokes.setup: Looking for the missing dog in the backyard
jokes.punchline: Reveals Homer has been digging a tunnel to steal Flanders' food
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist reveal. The specificity of 'Flanders's fridge' and the casual way it's mentioned makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You jackass. You lost our dog! Oh, my God. I forgot to feed my jackass.
jokes.setup: Marge calls Homer a jackass for losing the dog
jokes.punchline: Homer takes it literally and remembers his actual donkey
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection that works on multiple levels. Homer's obliviousness to the insult while caring about his actual jackass is perfect.

jokes.text: I love that dog, but that is one long, stupid name.
jokes.setup: Calling for Santa's Little Helper
jokes.punchline: Meta-commentary on the show's unnecessarily long dog name
jokes.explanation: Nice meta-moment acknowledging something fans have likely thought. Bart's perspective makes it feel natural.

jokes.text: A lot of good backups. How pregnant is the raccoon?
jokes.setup: Animal shelter lists available animals including a pregnant raccoon
jokes.punchline: Homer seriously considers a raccoon as a dog replacement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - treating wildly inappropriate animals as reasonable pet options. The pregnancy detail adds absurdity.

jokes.text: Good, Marge. Get their price down.
jokes.setup: Marge rejects getting a raccoon
jokes.punchline: Homer thinks she's negotiating rather than refusing
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing Homer's single-minded focus on getting any pet cheaply.

jokes.text: You cried when they canceled that show you hated. Goofing on it made me feel wise.
jokes.setup: Lisa points out Homer cries about everything
jokes.punchline: Homer reveals he only liked mocking shows, not watching them
jokes.explanation: Insightful character moment. Homer's admission about feeling wise through criticism is both funny and psychologically accurate.

jokes.text: And he didn't pee on the floor. For me, that's a perfect day.
jokes.setup: Finding Santa's Little Helper alive and well
jokes.punchline: Homer's incredibly low standards for pet behavior
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Homer's low expectations. The casual delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I believe the word is idiote.
jokes.setup: Marge calls Homer an idiot
jokes.punchline: Homer 'corrects' her with a pretentious French pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer pseudo-intellectualism. The correction that isn't really a correction is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Whoa, now I've got to buy a corpse wagon. Well, that's how they get you.
jokes.setup: Black plague creates corpses in his village
jokes.punchline: Homer treats medieval catastrophe like a mobile game cash grab
jokes.explanation: Brilliant modern/medieval juxtaposition. 'That's how they get you' is perfectly delivered commentary on mobile gaming.

jokes.text: ♪ Gonna drink ammonia ♪ 'Cause it tastes like cheese
jokes.setup: Homer singing while drinking something
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's drinking cleaning products because of their taste
jokes.explanation: Characteristic Homer food confusion but potentially too dark even for The Simpsons. The singing delivery helps.

jokes.text: A minstrel has composed a roundelay mocking me?
jokes.setup: Homer hearing his own singing
jokes.punchline: Interprets his modern song as medieval entertainment mocking him
jokes.explanation: Clever blending of Homer's gaming immersion with reality. The medieval vocabulary ('roundelay') shows his deep engagement.

jokes.text: That's short for 'Rrright you are.'
jokes.setup: Dog makes a sound
jokes.punchline: Homer provides an absurd translation
jokes.explanation: Minor character moment showing Homer's tendency to anthropomorphize. Not particularly strong but consistent.

jokes.text: How are they his treats if I paid for them?
jokes.setup: Lisa accuses Homer of eating dog treats
jokes.punchline: Homer's ownership logic overrides pet care
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - applying ownership rules to pet care. The economy and character integration are excellent.

jokes.text: Bongo?! I told you never to ever talk about Bongo. Ever!
jokes.setup: Grampa mentions 'Bongo'
jokes.punchline: Homer's explosive reaction to what seems like an innocent name
jokes.explanation: Great emotional escalation that sets up the backstory. Homer's overreaction creates intrigue about this mysterious past.

jokes.text: He's still my dog! We got him years ago. I don't know what you just said, but it's wrong!
jokes.setup: Homer getting emotional about the past
jokes.punchline: Incoherent emotional responses showing his unresolved trauma
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's emotional immaturity when confronting painful memories.

jokes.text: Anyway, Homer and that dog went together like Christmas and suicidal thoughts.
jokes.setup: Grampa describing Homer's bond with his dog
jokes.punchline: Unexpectedly dark comparison about holiday depression
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor that subverts expectations. The juxtaposition of heartwarming bond with seasonal depression is perfectly crafted.

jokes.text: Cancer clusters are random occurrences.
jokes.setup: Burns giving away promotional toys to children
jokes.punchline: Toy programmed to deliver nuclear industry talking points to kids
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of corporate PR targeting children. The matter-of-fact delivery of serious environmental issues as toy dialogue is inspired.

jokes.text: Those toys are people bribes, you good-hearted little brat.
jokes.setup: Young Homer offers his toy to Bongo
jokes.punchline: Dog apparently understands corporate manipulation better than humans
jokes.explanation: Clever reversal where the dog shows more social awareness than humans. Good satire of corporate manipulation.

jokes.text: Once I hold your dying pet in my arms and feel his wagging tail go stiff.
jokes.setup: Burns pretending to be gracious about the dog bite
jokes.punchline: Reveals his sadistic plan in disturbingly specific terms
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns villainy - the specific detail about the tail going stiff is both disturbing and darkly funny. Excellent character work.

jokes.text: Uh, sir, you are aware you're at a PR event for children.
jokes.setup: Burns just threatened to kill a child's pet
jokes.punchline: Gentle reminder of the inappropriate setting
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man response to Burns' excess. The understatement makes Burns' behavior even more outrageous.

jokes.text: So, Lady Godiva gets a haircut, right, and... Kids? Uh, no problem. I'll just cut to my clean material.
jokes.setup: Krusty starting what's clearly an adult joke
jokes.punchline: Realizes he's performing for children and tries to pivot
jokes.explanation: Good Krusty character moment showing his inappropriate default material. The awkward pivot is relatable performer anxiety.

jokes.text: Farmer's daughter, no. A man from Nantucket, no. Bring out the monkey!
jokes.setup: Krusty trying to find appropriate material
jokes.punchline: Realizes all his backup material is also inappropriate, defaults to physical comedy
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Krusty's entire repertoire is adult-oriented. The references are classically inappropriate jokes.

jokes.text: Next time I'm getting a smaller monkey.
jokes.setup: Monkey apparently attacks Krusty
jokes.punchline: Practical solution to being mauled by entertainment
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy payoff. Krusty's practical reaction to the chaos is characteristically professional.

jokes.text: Course now, he's just stupid and fat.
jokes.setup: Describing young Homer as innocent and chubby
jokes.punchline: Brutal assessment of adult Homer's character development
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa cruelty delivered matter-of-factly. The progression from 'innocent and chubby' to 'stupid and fat' is brutally efficient.

jokes.text: When God closes a door, he opens a gun shop.
jokes.setup: Variation on the common saying about doors and windows
jokes.punchline: Twisted into something much darker about violence and opportunity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of inspirational cliché into dark commentary. Perfect economy and structure with memorable quotability.

jokes.text: Wait. How could you know what Herman said? You were driving away. He wrote a memoir.
jokes.setup: Lisa catches a plot hole in Grampa's story
jokes.punchline: Absurd explanation that Herman documented this random moment
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor acknowledging storytelling conventions. The memoir explanation is absurdist enough to work.

jokes.text: A hole next to the seat to hold my cup? That's a stupid idea. Holding cups are what thighs are for.
jokes.setup: Young Homer suggests cup holders in cars
jokes.punchline: Grampa dismisses what became standard car feature
jokes.explanation: Great historical irony - young Homer accidentally invents cup holders. Grampa's dismissal adds generational humor.

jokes.text: Are you sure the Big Brother Program couldn't find me anybody else? Yeah, well, you were my last pick, too, okay?
jokes.setup: Lou complaining about being paired with Wiggum
jokes.punchline: Mutual dissatisfaction with the pairing
jokes.explanation: Decent character comedy establishing Lou and Wiggum's relationship dynamic. Not particularly original but functional.

jokes.text: Well, then I'll be a spokesman for my people, like Ralph Abernathy. Ralph, I like that name.
jokes.setup: Lou mentions civil rights leader Ralph Abernathy
jokes.punchline: Wiggum only focuses on the name for his future son
jokes.explanation: Nice origin story for Ralph Wiggum's name. Shows Wiggum's selective attention and provides series continuity.

jokes.text: He'd go to jail and stay there 'cause he loves the easy life
jokes.setup: Describing Homer's Monopoly strategy
jokes.punchline: Homer prefers jail because it's less demanding
jokes.explanation: Good character insight about Homer's laziness extending even to board games. Relatable gaming observation.

jokes.text: Now, son, a lot of people are cuckoo till you need something from them.
jokes.setup: Young Homer commenting that Miss Viola is crazy
jokes.punchline: Pragmatic wisdom about tolerating eccentricity when convenient
jokes.explanation: Excellent cynical wisdom that's both funny and truthful. Great economy and memorable phrasing.

jokes.text: Gosh, Miss Viola, I want you and your husband to know how much I appreciate this.
jokes.setup: Grampa thanking Miss Viola for taking the dog
jokes.punchline: Implies she's living alone with multiple pets but he assumes she has a husband
jokes.explanation: Minor gag about crazy cat lady stereotype. Works but isn't particularly strong or memorable.

jokes.text: I wasn't sure if a six-year-old could even understand the concept. What? No. No! No! Bongo! Turns out, he caught on pretty quick.
jokes.setup: Grampa wondering if Homer could understand losing the dog forever
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate devastating reaction proves he understands perfectly
jokes.explanation: Well-structured emotional payoff. The understatement of 'caught on pretty quick' contrasts perfectly with the heartbreaking reaction.

jokes.text: I can walk into McDonalds, order soup, and they'll make it.
jokes.setup: Burns asserting his power to threaten Grampa
jokes.punchline: Absurd example of power - making McDonald's serve soup
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns power flex. The McDonald's soup example is specific enough to be funny while showing his disconnect from reality.

jokes.text: Don't make me look into those pitiless black eyes and say, 'Daddy didn't bring you a friend-friend to rip-rip!'
jokes.setup: Miss Viola threatening Burns with her dogs
jokes.punchline: Baby talk applied to horrific violence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant juxtaposition of childish pet language with graphic violence. The repetition ('friend-friend', 'rip-rip') makes it funnier and more disturbing.

jokes.text: Dropping a horse on a church? No! Breaking a man's spirit. Mine. Well, I guess I could always drop a horse on a church afterward.
jokes.setup: Grampa trying to redirect Burns' evil impulses
jokes.punchline: Burns agrees but still wants to do the horse thing later
jokes.explanation: Great character comedy showing Burns' dedication to random acts of villainy. The horse-church combination is memorably absurd.

jokes.text: But those satanic Snoopys always found their way in.
jokes.setup: Grampa describing Burns' attack dogs
jokes.punchline: Compares vicious guard dogs to beloved cartoon character
jokes.explanation: Excellent juxtaposition of 'satanic' with 'Snoopys'. The contrast between vicious reality and innocent appearance is well-crafted.

jokes.text: Just house slippers and the most humiliating tie there is: bolo.
jokes.setup: Burns listing his humiliation of Grampa
jokes.punchline: Considers bolo tie the ultimate fashion humiliation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Burns' fashion snobbery. The specificity of the bolo tie as peak humiliation is funny.

jokes.text: You're probably wondering how I got rabies. Well... I'm not hungry anymore.
jokes.setup: Homer casually mentioning rabies
jokes.punchline: Young Homer's loss of appetite from the non sequitur
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor showing the effect of Grampa's disturbing stories on young Homer's appetite.

jokes.text: Grampa always hated Bongo and loved money so he sold Bongo to a wicked farm witch just so he could hang out with a bunch of rich dogs and kidnapped Santa
jokes.setup: Homer offering his version of events
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd accusations that make no sense
jokes.explanation: Shows Homer's childish perspective and inability to understand complex situations. The escalation works but becomes incoherent.

jokes.text: Gee, my story doesn't make much sense except the Santa part.
jokes.setup: Homer's incoherent version of events
jokes.punchline: He thinks the Santa kidnapping is the believable part
jokes.explanation: Good payoff showing Homer's skewed priorities and logic. The irony of considering the most absurd part believable is well-done.

jokes.text: Well, that's just the way dogs are. The most disloyal, unfaithful creatures God ever made.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining his cynicism about dogs
jokes.punchline: Complete misunderstanding of dog loyalty - they're famously loyal
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal of common knowledge about dogs. Shows how trauma warped Homer's perspective.

jokes.text: He still had my sweatshirt.
jokes.setup: Looking at Christmas card from Miss Viola
jokes.punchline: Reveals Bongo never forgot Homer after all
jokes.explanation: Excellent emotional payoff and callback. The simplicity and economy make the moment more powerful.

jokes.text: Unlike those gerbils of yours we gave away. Oh, they were fickle as hell.
jokes.setup: Emotional moment about Bongo's loyalty
jokes.punchline: Grampa deflates the moment with cynicism about other pets
jokes.explanation: Typical Grampa undermining of sentimental moments. The casual cruelty is character-appropriate but not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: Just because I put you in a home where they feed you dog food?
jokes.setup: Homer wondering why Grampa thought he loved the dog more
jokes.punchline: Casually reveals he's mistreating Grampa
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about elder abuse delivered casually. Shows Homer's obliviousness to his own cruelty.

jokes.text: Hold your arms like you're carrying a wedding cake. What flavor? It doesn't matter. If it doesn't matter, then I call Snickers.
jokes.setup: Grampa teaching Homer how to hug
jokes.punchline: Homer's literal interpretation leads to candy bar preference
jokes.explanation: Good Homer literalism that derails a touching moment. The Snickers choice is randomly specific in a funny way.

jokes.text: Everything's a flavor in the kitchens of today.
jokes.setup: Defending his Snickers cake choice
jokes.punchline: Overly philosophical justification for absurd food preference
jokes.explanation: Minor Homer moment showing his ability to make grand statements about trivial things.

jokes.text: Think of the economy as a car and the rich man as the driver. If you don't give the driver all the money, he'll drive you over a cliff.
jokes.setup: Burns explaining the fiscal cliff
jokes.punchline: Transparently self-serving economic metaphor that makes no sense
jokes.explanation: Good political satire showing how the wealthy frame economic arguments. Burns' transparent self-interest is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Furthermore, rich people feel things more deeply than the common man.
jokes.setup: Burns continuing his economic justification
jokes.punchline: Absurd claim about rich people's superior emotional capacity
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of Burns' delusional self-justification. The absurdity of the claim makes it effective satire.

jokes.text: I have a progressive proposal to let into this country 200 grimy Irishmen a year. I've got lots of potatoes that need peeling and stables to be mucked out.
jokes.setup: Burns claiming to have progressive immigration ideas
jokes.punchline: Reveals blatantly exploitative 19th-century mindset
jokes.explanation: Excellent Burns characterization showing his complete disconnect from modern values. The 'progressive' framing makes it more satirical.

jokes.text: You just said Marco Rubio is a pink handkerchief.
jokes.setup: Burns apparently making another political statement
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's speaking gibberish that sounds like political commentary
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment showing how political discourse can become meaningless. The specific translation is memorably weird.

jokes.text: Execute the cameraman.
jokes.setup: Burns' disastrous public appearance
jokes.punchline: Characteristically extreme overreaction to bad publicity
jokes.explanation: Typical Burns extreme reaction. Expected but still effective for the character.

tmdb_overview: Grampa has to move into the Simpsons house following a fire at the retirement home, and on moving day, Homer 'throws his back out' and 'rests' at home. He later finds an advert for a video game, Villageville on his myPad, and starts playing. During that time, Homer loses Santa's Little Helper. When the family find him, Homer doesn't seem to care because he's "not a dog person". Grampa later tells the rest of the family the story of Homer's childhood companion, pet dog Bongo.
